Course Content

title: Orientation in This Course
text:
Welcome! This course is divided into twelve thematic sections that will gradually introduce you to the world of data analysis with R or Python.
On this platform you will find everything in one place: clear explanations, directly executable code examples for experimentation, and short knowledge tests after each chapter to consolidate what you have learned.
You have full freedom of choice: using the button in the top right corner, you can switch between the programming languages R and Python at any time. The subject matter is identical ‚Äî you decide which tool you want to work with.
In addition, all lecture slides and Markdown files with the same content are linked so that you can also work through the materials independently offline.

title: Orientation in This Course
text:
Welcome! This course is divided into twelve thematic sections that will gradually introduce you to the world of data analysis with R or Python.
On this platform you will find everything in one place: clear explanations, directly executable code examples for experimentation, and short knowledge tests after each chapter to consolidate what you have learned.
You have full freedom of choice: using the button in the top right corner, you can switch between the programming languages R and Python at any time. The subject matter is identical ‚Äî you decide which tool you want to work with.
In addition, all lecture slides and Markdown files with the same content are linked so that you can also work through the materials independently offline.

title: Orientation in This Course
section: Programming Languages and Their Working Environments
text:
Programming Languages and Their Working Environments
Before we start, it is important to understand the difference between the programming language itself and the program in which we write.

1. The Language: R and Python
R and Python are the ‚Äúengines‚Äù behind your analysis. For your computer to understand what you want from it, you need to download the language as base software.

Python: Installed as a program from python.org.
R: Installed as ‚ÄúBase R‚Äù from the CRAN network.

Important: You will almost never open these programs directly. They ‚Äúrest‚Äù in the background of your operating system and wait for commands.


2. The Tool: The IDE (RStudio & VS Code)
To actually write code, we use an IDE (Integrated Development Environment). This is your actual control center.

RStudio is the standard environment for R.
VS Code is the most popular environment for Python.

title: Orientation in This Course
section: What Do You Need for Setup?
text:
What Do You Need for Setup?
If you later want to work on your own computer, this is the correct order:

Install the engine: Download Python or R (provides the ‚Äúlanguage‚Äù).
Set up the workspace: Install an IDE like RStudio or VS Code (your workstation).
Unpack the tools: Install so-called ‚Äúpackages‚Äù within the IDE. These are extensions for specific tasks, such as drawing biological diagrams.







TipOur Tip for Getting Started



To work on the exercises on this website, no installed RStudio or VS Code is required. Our interactive windows already have everything ‚Äúon board‚Äù. However, we recommend that you familiarize yourself with these programs during the course, as they will be your daily companions in later practice!

title: Orientation in This Course
text:
Working with Markdown
In this course we do not use ordinary text documents or pure program code. In addition to this website, we work with Markdown files as an offline alternative.
In science, reproducibility (traceability) is crucial. Markdown allows us to do so-called Literate Programming: * We write our text (explanations, hypotheses). * We embed the actual code (R or Python) directly within it. * We let the computer render the document (in RStudio often via the Knit button).
The result is a finished document (HTML, PDF, or Word) in which the text, the code, and the resulting graphics are perfectly combined.

The Structure of a File

The text: Regular text with simple formatting (**bold**, *italic*, # Heading).
The code chunks: Blocks that begin with three backticks. This is where the actual code is executed. It is recommended to name each chunk ‚Äî this keeps your document tidy and greatly facilitates troubleshooting.
The YAML header: The area at the very top between the --- lines for title and author.








NoteLanguage-Specific Introduction



Depending on whether you have switched to ‚ÄúR‚Äù or ‚ÄúPython‚Äù above, you will now see a more detailed introduction to the respective environment below.

title: Orientation in This Course
text:
Focus: R and RStudio
R is a powerful language for statistical data analysis. We recommend that you also take the introductory courses at DataCamp to get a feel for the syntax. RStudio is an ‚ÄúIntegrated Development Environment‚Äù (IDE) that helps you write code, visualize data, and save results.

The RStudio Interface
The interface is divided into four main areas:

Source (top left): This is where you write your scripts and Markdown files. To create a new file, click on the New File icon (green plus) at the top left. Everything written here can be saved.
Console (bottom left): Code is executed immediately here. For example, type 3 + 4 and press Enter ‚Äî the result appears immediately. The console is ideal for commands that you do not need to save permanently.
Environment (top right): All variables, lists, and datasets that you have created and defined during your session are displayed here.
Files/Plots/Help (bottom right): Here you manage your files, view generated graphics, or search the help for functions (e.g., with ?matrix).




The main areas of RStudio




First Steps in Programming

Assignments: Values are loaded into variables with <- or = (e.g., a <- 10). The variable then appears in the Environment.
R as a calculator: R is excellent for mathematical calculations. You can calculate directly with variables:

#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 300
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  textAreaInput("code", "R Chunk: Mathematical Operations", value = "x <- 5\ny <- 3\nSum <- x + y\nSum * 5", width = '100%', height = '100px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output) {
  observeEvent(input$run, { output$out <- renderPrint({ eval(parse(text = input$code)) }) })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)

Case Sensitivity: R is extremely picky. hello is a different variable from Hello.
Getting help: Use the documentation directly in RStudio or online resources:

guru99 R Tutorial
Babraham Bioinformatics
R for Data Science

Workspace: Use ls() to list your variables and rm(a) to delete them ‚Äî Caution: rm() deletes irreversibly, so use it carefully! Your entire environment can be saved as an .RData file and restored in a later session with load("MyEnvironment.RData").
Working Directory: Via the Files -> More -> Set As Working Directory tab, you define which folder R should look in for your files.


Helpful Tips in the Editor

Autocomplete: In RStudio, quotation marks "" and brackets () usually appear automatically in pairs. This helps you avoid syntax errors caused by forgotten characters.
History: Using the arrow keys (up/down) you can scroll through your most recently used commands in the console.
Comments: Everything after a # is ignored by R. Use this generously to explain your code to yourself and others.



Variables & Assignments
We can load values (numbers or text) into variables. In R, the arrow <- is traditionally used for this, but = is also possible.
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 300
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  textAreaInput("code", "R Chunk: Variables & Assignments", value = "a <- \"Hello world!\"\nhello = \"Hello world!\"\nprint(a)\nprint(hello)", width = '100%', height = '120px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output) {
  observeEvent(input$run, { output$out <- renderPrint({ eval(parse(text = input$code)) }) })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)llo = "Hello world!"

title: Orientation in This Course
text:
Python and VS Code
Python is a versatile and widely used programming language that is particularly well-suited for data analysis, machine learning, and scientific computing. We recommend that you also take the introductory courses at DataCamp to get a feel for the syntax. VS Code is a flexible development environment (IDE) that can be optimally adapted for Python through extensions.

The VS Code Interface
The interface is divided into four main areas:

Editor (center): This is where you write your scripts and notebooks. To create a new file, click on the New File icon at the top left in the Explorer. Everything written here can be saved. The file extensions determine in which programming language you are writing and how the file can be used (.py/.ipynb for Python, .r for R, .md for Markdown).
Terminal (bottom): Code is executed immediately here and files can be launched. Connections to clusters, communication with GitHub, or searching for files also work via the terminal. The terminal can be opened, for example, at the top right (3rd icon from the right).
Explorer (left): Here you manage your files and folders. These are either stored locally on your computer or on a cluster.
Control Center (far left): Here you can, for example, search documents for a keyword, connect and manage GitHub repositories, debug, install extensions, and manage remote connections (e.g., clusters).




The main areas of VS Code




First Steps in Programming

Assignments: In Python, values are loaded into variables with = (e.g., a = 10). Unlike R, there is no <- in Python.
Python as a calculator: Python is excellent for mathematical calculations. You can calculate directly with variables:

#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 300
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import io, contextlib

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text_area("code", "Python Chunk: Mathematical Operations", value="x = 5\ny = 3\nSum = x + y\nprint(Sum * 5)", width='100%', height='100px'),
    ui.input_action_button("run", "Run Code", class_="btn-success"),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("out")
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    @reactive.event(input.run)
    def out():
        f = io.StringIO()
        with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f):
            exec(input.code(), {})
        return f.getvalue()

app = App(app_ui, server)

Case Sensitivity: Python is just as picky as R. hello is a different variable from Hello.



Getting Help:
Use the documentation directly in VS Code or online resources: Python Documentation W3Schools Python Tutorial Python for Data Science Handbook

Workspace: Use dir() to list all current variables and objects, and del a to delete a variable ‚Äî Caution: del deletes irreversibly, so use it carefully!
Working Directory: With import os and os.getcwd() you can display the current directory; with os.chdir("path/to/folder") you change it.



Helpful Tips in the Editor

Autocomplete: VS Code automatically suggests functions, variable names, and brackets. Quotation marks "" and brackets () often appear automatically in pairs ‚Äî this helps avoid syntax errors.
History: In the terminal you can scroll through your most recently used commands using the arrow keys (up/down).
Comments: Everything after a # is ignored by Python ‚Äî just like in R. Use comments generously to explain your code.



Variables & Assignments
We can load values (numbers or text) into variables. In Python, = is used exclusively for assignments.
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 300
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import io, contextlib

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text_area("code", "Python Chunk: Variables & Assignments", value='a = "Hello world!"\nhello = "Hello world!"\nprint(a)\nprint(hello)', width='100%', height='120px'),
    ui.input_action_button("run", "Run Code", class_="btn-success"),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("out")
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    @reactive.event(input.run)
    def out():
        f = io.StringIO()
        with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f):
            exec(input.code(), {})
        return f.getvalue()

app = App(app_ui, server)

title: Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation
text:
Week 1 Progress
      Loading‚Ä¶
    
    ‚Üê Dashboard
  
  
    
  
  Complete either the R or the Python track to reach 100%
Let‚Äôs explore how data is organised and displayed in R and Python! We will first introduce the basic concepts of datatypes and then how you can manipulate this structures. At the end of each section you will find a quiz to practise the learned content.

title: Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation
Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation
Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation
Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation
Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation
text:
5 ‚Äì Datatype: Dataframes
Dataframes are like matrices, but can contain various data types in different columns. They are created with data.frame():

#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 270
library(shiny)
CHUNK_ID <- "w1_c19"
ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$script('Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("chunkRun",function(m){window.parent.postMessage({type:"chunkDone",chunkId:m.chunkId},"*");});'),
  textAreaInput("code", "Creating a Dataframe", value = "Name <- c('Ben', 'Alice', 'Daniel', 'Charlie')\nYear <- c(1990, 1992, 1995, 1993)\nOption <- c('Biology','Physics', 'Chemistry', 'Mathematics')\nStudents <- data.frame(First_Name = Name, Year = Year, Options = Option)\nStudents", width = '100%', height = '150px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output, session) {
  observeEvent(input$run, {
    session$sendCustomMessage("chunkRun", list(chunkId = CHUNK_ID))
    output$out <- renderPrint({
      exprs <- parse(text = input$code)
      for (e in exprs) { res <- withVisible(eval(e)); if (!is.null(res$value) && res$visible) print(res$value) }
    })
  })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)

Dataframes can be indexed similarly to lists and matrices:

#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 280
library(shiny)
CHUNK_ID <- "w1_c20"
ui <- fluidPage(
  tags$script('Shiny.addCustomMessageHandler("chunkRun",function(m){window.parent.postMessage({type:"chunkDone",chunkId:m.chunkId},"*");});'),
  textAreaInput("code", "Dataframe Access", value = "Name <- c('Ben', 'Alice', 'Daniel', 'Charlie')\nYear <- c(1990, 1992, 1995, 1993)\nOption <- c('Biology','Physics', 'Chemistry', 'Mathematics')\nStudents <- data.frame(First_Name = Name, Year = Year, Options = Option)\n\nStudents$Year          # Column 'Year'\nStudents[['Year']]     # identical\nStudents[, 2]          # identical\nStudents$Options[3]    # 3rd element of 'Options'\nStudents[2:3, 3]       # Elements 2-3 of column 3", width = '100%', height = '175px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output, session) {
  observeEvent(input$run, {
    session$sendCustomMessage("chunkRun", list(chunkId = CHUNK_ID))
    output$out <- renderPrint({
      exprs <- parse(text = input$code)
      for (e in exprs) { res <- withVisible(eval(e)); if (!is.null(res$value) && res$visible) print(res$value) }
    })
  })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)

There are important differences between dataframes and matrices. Note especially what Df[4] returns compared to Mat[4]:

Key difference: For a matrix, Mat[4] returns the 4th element column-wise. For a dataframe, Df[4] returns the 4th column. Always use Df[2,3] or Df.iloc[2,3] for single elements.

Dataframes can be merged using merge():

merge() by default performs an Inner Join ‚Äì only rows with matching keys in both dataframes appear in the result. Use all=TRUE to keep all rows (Outer Join).


üí° If you need more information on a specific function, type ?data.frame or help(merge) in the Console.

title: Week 1: Basic Data Structures & Data Manipulation

text:
EXERCISES - Datatypes
Answer the questions correctly to save your progress. You can use the code sandboxes to experiment!


Exercise 1: Vectors

  QUIZ: VECTORS
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 220
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  textAreaInput("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Vectors", value = "# Try anything here!\n", width = '100%', height = '100px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output) {
  observeEvent(input$run, {
    output$out <- renderPrint({
      exprs <- parse(text = input$code)
      for (e in exprs) { res <- withVisible(eval(e)); if (!is.null(res$value) && res$visible) print(res$value) }
    })
  })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What does length(c(9, 3, 7, 2)) return?
    2
    4
    9
    1
    
  
  
    Question 2: Why does sum(c(TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, TRUE)) return 3?
    Because the vector has 3 elements
    Because R treats TRUE as 1 and FALSE as 0 ‚Äì sum() therefore counts the TRUE values
    Because sum() only counts the first three elements
    
  
  
    Question 3: What does y[y < 5] return when y <- c(9, 3, 7, 2)?
    9 7
    3 2
    TRUE FALSE TRUE TRUE
    
  
  
    Question 4: What happens when data types are mixed in a vector, e.g. c(TRUE, "FALSE", 3)?
    R throws an error
    Each element keeps its own type
    All elements are converted to the lowest common type (here: character)
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS
  





Exercise 2: Matrices

  QUIZ: MATRICES
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 220
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  textAreaInput("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Matrices", value = "# Create your own matrix here!\nX <- matrix(1:6, nrow=2, ncol=3, byrow=TRUE)\nX", width = '100%', height = '100px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output) {
  observeEvent(input$run, {
    output$out <- renderPrint({
      exprs <- parse(text = input$code)
      for (e in exprs) { res <- withVisible(eval(e)); if (!is.null(res$value) && res$visible) print(res$value) }
    })
  })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What does dim(X) return for matrix(1:6, nrow=2, ncol=3)?
    6
    2 3
    3 2
    
  
  
    Question 2: What is the difference between cbind() and rbind()?
    No difference ‚Äì both do the same thing
    cbind() joins vectors as columns, rbind() as rows
    cbind() only works for numbers, rbind() for all types
    
  
  
    Question 3: What does X[2, ] return?
    The element in row 2, column 0
    All elements of the second row
    All elements of the second column
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS
  





Exercise 3: Lists

  QUIZ: LISTS
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 220
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  textAreaInput("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Lists", value = "List1 <- list(color = c('blue', 'red'), size = 5, state = TRUE)\n# What is the difference?\nList1['color']\nList1[['color']]", width = '100%', height = '100px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output) {
  observeEvent(input$run, {
    output$out <- renderPrint({
      exprs <- parse(text = input$code)
      for (e in exprs) { res <- withVisible(eval(e)); if (!is.null(res$value) && res$visible) print(res$value) }
    })
  })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What is the difference between List1["color"] and List1[["color"]]?
    No difference ‚Äì both return the same value
    List1["color"] returns a list of length 1, List1[["color"]] returns the content (the vector) directly
    List1[["color"]] returns a list, List1["color"] the content
    
  
  
    Question 2: How do you access the second position of the first element of List2 <- list(c("blue", "red"), 5, TRUE)?
    List2[1][2]
    List2[[1]][2]
    List2$1[2]
    
  
  
    Question 3: How do you delete the third element from a list?
    List2[3] <- FALSE
    List2[[3]] <- NULL
    rm(List2[[3]])
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS
  





Exercise 4: Dataframes

  QUIZ: DATAFRAMES
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 250
library(shiny)
ui <- fluidPage(
  textAreaInput("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Dataframes", value = "Product_name <- c('orange', 'strawberry', 'broccoli', 'blueberry', 'cucumber')\nFruit <- c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE)\nColor <- c('orange', 'red', 'green', 'blue', 'green')\ndf <- data.frame(Product_name, Fruit, Color)\ndf", width = '100%', height = '135px'),
  actionButton("run", "Run Code", class = "btn-success"),
  verbatimTextOutput("out")
)
server <- function(input, output) {
  observeEvent(input$run, {
    output$out <- renderPrint({
      exprs <- parse(text = input$code)
      for (e in exprs) { res <- withVisible(eval(e)); if (!is.null(res$value) && res$visible) print(res$value) }
    })
  })
}
shinyApp(ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What is the most important difference between a dataframe and a matrix?
    Dataframes can be larger than matrices
    Dataframes can contain different data types in different columns, matrices only one type
    Matrices always have column names, dataframes do not
    
  
  
    Question 2: How many fruits does the vector Fruit <- c(TRUE, TRUE, FALSE, TRUE, FALSE) contain? Which function do you use?
    2, with length(Fruit)
    3, with sum(Fruit)
    5, with sum(Fruit)
    
  
  
    Question 3: What does merge(Students, Full_Names) do by default (i.e. without additional parameters)?
    All rows of both dataframes are merged, missing values filled with NA
    Only rows that appear in both dataframes are merged (Inner Join)
    The dataframes are stacked on top of each other (rows)
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS

text:
EXERCISES - Datatypes
Answer the questions correctly to save your progress. You can use the code sandboxes to experiment!


Exercise 1: Lists & Arrays

  QUIZ: LISTS & ARRAYS
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 230
#| packages: [numpy]
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import io, contextlib, ast
import numpy as np

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text_area("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Lists & Arrays", value="import numpy as np\n# Try anything here!\n", width='100%', height='100px'),
    ui.input_action_button("run", "Run Code", class_="btn-success"),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("out")
)
def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    @reactive.event(input.run)
    async def out():
        f = io.StringIO(); ns = {}
        with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f):
            try:
                tree = ast.parse(input.code())
                for node in tree.body:
                    if isinstance(node, ast.Expr):
                        val = eval(compile(ast.Expression(node.value),'<s>','eval'), ns)
                        if val is not None: print(val)
                    else: exec(compile(ast.Module([node],[]),'<s>','exec'), ns)
            except Exception as e: print(f"Error: {e}")
        return f.getvalue()
app = App(app_ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What does len([9, 3, 7, 2]) return?
    2
    4
    9
    
  
  
    Question 2: Python uses 0-based indices. How do you access the third element of y = [9, 3, 7, 2]?
    y[3]
    y[2]
    y[1]
    
  
  
    Question 3: Why does sum(np.array([True, False, True, True])) return 3?
    Because the array has 3 elements
    Because Python treats True as 1 and False as 0 ‚Äì sum() therefore counts the True values
    Because sum() only counts the first three elements
    
  
  
    Question 4: What does y[y < 5] return when y = np.array([9, 3, 7, 2])?
    [9, 7]
    [3, 2]
    [True, False, True, True]
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS
  





Exercise 2: Matrices

  QUIZ: MATRICES
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 230
#| packages: [numpy]
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import io, contextlib, ast
import numpy as np

app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text_area("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Matrices", value="import numpy as np\nX = np.arange(1, 7).reshape(2, 3)\nprint(X)\nprint(X.shape)", width='100%', height='100px'),
    ui.input_action_button("run", "Run Code", class_="btn-success"),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("out")
)
def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    @reactive.event(input.run)
    async def out():
        f = io.StringIO(); ns = {}
        with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f):
            try:
                tree = ast.parse(input.code())
                for node in tree.body:
                    if isinstance(node, ast.Expr):
                        val = eval(compile(ast.Expression(node.value),'<s>','eval'), ns)
                        if val is not None: print(val)
                    else: exec(compile(ast.Module([node],[]),'<s>','exec'), ns)
            except Exception as e: print(f"Error: {e}")
        return f.getvalue()
app = App(app_ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What does X.shape return for np.arange(1,7).reshape(2,3)?
    6
    (2, 3)
    (3, 2)
    
  
  
    Question 2: What is the difference between np.column_stack() and np.row_stack()?
    No difference
    column_stack() joins vectors as columns, row_stack() as rows
    column_stack() only works for numbers
    
  
  
    Question 3: What does X[1, :] return?
    The element at row 1, column 0
    All elements of the second row (index 1)
    All elements of the second column
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS
  





Exercise 3: Dictionaries

  QUIZ: DICTIONARIES
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 220
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import io, contextlib, ast
app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text_area("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì Dictionaries", value="dict1 = {'color': ['blue', 'red'], 'size': 5, 'state': True}\nprint(type(dict1['color']))\nprint(dict1['color'][1])", width='100%', height='100px'),
    ui.input_action_button("run", "Run Code", class_="btn-success"),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("out")
)
def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    @reactive.event(input.run)
    async def out():
        f = io.StringIO(); ns = {}
        with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f):
            try:
                tree = ast.parse(input.code())
                for node in tree.body:
                    if isinstance(node, ast.Expr):
                        val = eval(compile(ast.Expression(node.value),'<s>','eval'), ns)
                        if val is not None: print(val)
                    else: exec(compile(ast.Module([node],[]),'<s>','exec'), ns)
            except Exception as e: print(f"Error: {e}")
        return f.getvalue()
app = App(app_ui, server)
  
    Question 1: How do you access the value of key 'color' in dict1 = {'color': ['blue', 'red'], 'size': 5}?
    dict1[0]
    dict1['color']
    dict1.color
    
  
  
    Question 2: How do you access the second position of the value of 'color'?
    dict1['color'][2]
    dict1['color'][1]
    dict1[1]['color']
    
  
  
    Question 3: How do you delete key 'size' from a dictionary?
    dict1['size'] = None
    del dict1['size']
    dict1.remove('size')
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS
  





Exercise 4: DataFrames

  QUIZ: DATAFRAMES
  üß™ Try out your code here:
#| '!! shinylive warning !!': |
#|   shinylive does not work in self-contained HTML documents.
#|   Please set `embed-resources: false` in your metadata.
#| standalone: true
#| viewerHeight: 250
from shiny import App, ui, render, reactive
import io, contextlib, ast
app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_text_area("code", "Sandbox ‚Äì DataFrames", value="import pandas as pd\nProduct_name = ['orange', 'strawberry', 'broccoli', 'blueberry', 'cucumber']\nFruit = [True, True, False, True, False]\nColor = ['orange', 'red', 'green', 'blue', 'green']\ndf = pd.DataFrame({'Product_name': Product_name, 'Fruit': Fruit, 'Color': Color})\nprint(df)", width='100%', height='140px'),
    ui.input_action_button("run", "Run Code", class_="btn-success"),
    ui.output_text_verbatim("out")
)
def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    @reactive.event(input.run)
    async def out():
        f = io.StringIO(); ns = {}
        with contextlib.redirect_stdout(f):
            try:
                tree = ast.parse(input.code())
                for node in tree.body:
                    if isinstance(node, ast.Expr):
                        val = eval(compile(ast.Expression(node.value),'<s>','eval'), ns)
                        if val is not None: print(val)
                    else: exec(compile(ast.Module([node],[]),'<s>','exec'), ns)
            except Exception as e: print(f"Error: {e}")
        return f.getvalue()
app = App(app_ui, server)
  
    Question 1: What is the most important difference between a pandas DataFrame and a NumPy matrix?
    DataFrames can be larger
    DataFrames can contain different data types in different columns, NumPy arrays only one type
    NumPy arrays always have column names
    
  
  
    Question 2: How many fruits does Fruit = [True, True, False, True, False] contain? Which function do you use?
    2, with len(Fruit)
    3, with sum(Fruit)
    5, with sum(Fruit)
    
  
  
    Question 3: What does pd.merge(Students, Full_Names) do by default?
    All rows of both DataFrames are merged, missing values filled with NaN
    Only rows that appear in both DataFrames are merged (Inner Join)
    The DataFrames are stacked on top of each other (rows)
    
  
  CHECK ANSWERS

text:
Offline Materials

Lecture Material
